target: ISS-701: PRED-090 NH TOA flux excess >0.3 W/m² assessment
reasoning: PRED-090 directly restates WIN-066 (NH heat excess asymmetry, verdict: Std Model Explains). The claimed +0.34 W/m² figure comes from Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS), which documents a decadal TREND of 0.34 ± 0.23 W/m² dec⁻¹ in the NH–SH absorbed solar radiation (ASR) difference over 2001–2024 — not a static hemispheric excess. The dome conflates a trend rate (W/m² per decade) with a static prediction (>0.3 W/m²), misrepresenting the nature of the observation. The paper was published in 2025, well before the prediction registration date of 2026-04-04, making this unambiguously post-hoc. Standard physics fully explains the asymmetry: decreased NH aerosols (China, US, Europe pollution reductions), Arctic sea-ice loss exposing darker surfaces, and increased water vapor absorption in the warming NH. The dome provides no radiation budget calculation connecting its geometry to any specific W/m² value — as noted in WIN-066, dome disc geometry with a local sun at 5,733 km actually predicts an inverse-square flux difference on the order of 90–270 W/m², hundreds of times larger than the observed 0.34 W/m²/decade trend.
testability_notes: The claim specifies >0.3 W/m² but conflates a decadal trend rate with a static hemispheric excess. CERES EBAF monthly composites for April 2026 will not be available until approximately July–August 2026 due to standard processing lag, making the April 2026 test window poorly defined. More fundamentally, a single month's TOA flux is dominated by seasonal and weather variability, not the multi-decadal ASR trend the dome is citing.
author_status_notes: Author marks as pending but the confirming data (Raghuraman et al. 2025 PNAS, CERES EBAF Ed4.2.1 through December 2024) was published before the April 2026 registration date. By the dome author's own framework distinguishing PROSPECTIVE from BACKTESTED, this is backtested.
time_sensitive_notes: Despite the April 2026 test window, no new data will be available this month. The assessment is based on existing published data which already covers the relevant measurements through 2024.
kernel_of_truth: The NH IS absorbing more solar radiation than the SH, and the trend is real and significant — Raghuraman et al. (2025) is a high-quality study using 24 years of CERES data. The dome correctly identifies a genuine geophysical phenomenon. However, (1) the dome provides no mechanism connecting its flat-disc geometry to this specific value, (2) the dome's own geometry predicts a far larger asymmetry by orders of magnitude, (3) the observation is a decadal TREND not a static excess, and (4) standard physics (aerosol changes, ice-albedo feedback, water vapor) provides complete causal explanation without requiring dome geometry.
data_sources: Raghuraman et al. (2025), 'Emerging hemispheric asymmetry of Earth's radiation', PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122
Stephens et al. (2012), Nature Geoscience, doi:10.1038/ngeo1580 — established hemispheric energy balance symmetry
CERES EBAF Ed4.2.1 TOA data product (through December 2024)

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome predicted >0.3 W/m² and the measured trend is 0.34 W/m² — that's a confirmed prediction
anticipated_objections.response: The 0.34 is a trend rate (W/m² per decade), not a static excess. The dome states a static >0.3 W/m², conflating two different quantities. Even accepting the numerical coincidence, the prediction was registered after Raghuraman et al. (2025) published the 0.34 figure — the number was publicly available before registration.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome's geometric asymmetry (NP at center, varying firmament height) naturally predicts hemispheric energy differences
anticipated_objections.response: Dome geometry does predict asymmetry, but of the wrong magnitude. A flat disc with local sun at 5,733 km produces inverse-square flux falloff giving ~90–270 W/m² hemispheric difference — hundreds of times larger than observed. The dome adopts the published number without deriving it from dome parameters.

anticipated_objections.objection: April 2026 data might show even stronger NH excess, confirming the prediction
anticipated_objections.response: Monthly TOA flux varies by ±2–5 W/m² due to seasonal and weather effects, dwarfing the 0.034 W/m²/year trend signal. A single month cannot confirm or falsify a multi-decadal trend. Additionally, CERES monthly composites for April 2026 will not be processed until mid-2026.
